name: Canada
tagline: From Indigenous homelands and New France to a confederated dominion that grew into an autonomous, multicultural nation.
summary: Canada's history begins with Indigenous peoples — First Nations, Inuit, and later the Métis — whose ancestors had occupied the land for thousands of years before European contact. Norse mariners reached Newfoundland around the year 1000, but sustained European settlement came with the French, who built New France around the St. Lawrence fur trade from 1608. Britain conquered New France in the Seven Years' War, confirmed by the Treaty of Paris in 1763, and governed a string of British North American colonies until they federated. On 1 July 1867 the British North America Act created the Dominion of Canada, a self-governing federation that swiftly expanded across the continent — an expansion built on railways, immigration, and the dispossession of Indigenous nations through treaties, reserves, and residential schools. Two world wars deepened Canadian autonomy, formalised by the 1931 Statute of Westminster, and the 1982 patriation of the constitution made Canada fully sovereign. Modern Canada is a bilingual, officially multicultural country still reckoning with the legacy of colonialism and the work of reconciliation.

founding.label: Confederation — the Dominion of Canada
founding.detail: Canada became a self-governing federation on 1 July 1867, when the British North America Act (passed by the United Kingdom Parliament and given royal assent on 29 March 1867) united the Province of Canada, Nova Scotia, and New Brunswick into one Dominion under the name of Canada. This founding rested on far deeper foundations: Indigenous peoples had lived across the land for millennia, and the French had built New France around the St. Lawrence from 1608 before the British conquest of 1763. Confederation created a country still tied to Britain; full autonomy came in stages. The Statute of Westminster (1931) granted Canada legislative independence, and the patriation of the constitution in 1982 — through the Constitution Act, 1982, and the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms — gave Canada the power to amend its own constitution without recourse to the British Parliament, completing its journey to full sovereignty.

quickFacts.label: Confederation
quickFacts.value: British North America Act, effective 1 July 1867

quickFacts.label: Founding provinces
quickFacts.value: Ontario, Quebec, Nova Scotia, New Brunswick

quickFacts.label: Constitution patriated
quickFacts.value: Constitution Act, 1982, with the Charter of Rights and Freedoms

quickFacts.label: Government
quickFacts.value: Federal parliamentary democracy and constitutional monarchy

quickFacts.label: Official languages
quickFacts.value: English and French; officially multicultural since 1971

eras.title: Indigenous peoples and First Nations before contact
eras.standfirst: For thousands of years before Europeans arrived, the land that became Canada was home to diverse Indigenous nations with their own languages, governments, trade networks, and ways of life.
body: Indigenous peoples have lived in what is now Canada for thousands of years. Archaeological evidence places the ancestors of First Nations across the continent by at least the end of the last Ice Age, with peoples spreading from coast to coast and into the Arctic over the following millennia. By the time of European contact, hundreds of distinct nations occupied the land, organised into many cultural and linguistic groups — from the Haudenosaunee (Iroquois) and Anishinaabe of the eastern woodlands to the Cree and Blackfoot of the plains, the diverse nations of the Pacific Northwest Coast, and the Inuit of the Arctic.

These societies were far from uniform. Some were mobile hunters and gatherers; others, such as the Haudenosaunee, were settled agriculturalists who grew corn, beans, and squash and lived in fortified longhouse villages. The peoples of the Northwest Coast built complex, hierarchical societies sustained by abundant salmon and cedar. Across the continent, sophisticated trade routes carried goods, ideas, and obligations over vast distances, and elaborate political and spiritual traditions governed relations among nations.

Norse sailors briefly reached the northern tip of Newfoundland around the year 1000, leaving the archaeological site of L'Anse aux Meadows — the first known European presence in the Americas — but this contact was fleeting and left no lasting settlement. Sustained encounter with Europeans began only at the close of the fifteenth century, with John Cabot's voyage of 1497 to the Atlantic coast. The arrival of Europeans would soon bring trade, alliance, and catastrophic epidemic disease that reshaped Indigenous societies long before large-scale settlement reached the interior.

events.title: Peopling of the continent
events.summary: The ancestors of First Nations were established across the Americas by the end of the last Ice Age, developing diverse societies, languages, and economies over thousands of years before European contact.

events.title: Norse arrival at L'Anse aux Meadows
events.summary: Norse explorers established a short-lived base at L'Anse aux Meadows on the northern tip of Newfoundland around the year 1000 — the first known European presence in the Americas, now a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

events.title: John Cabot reaches the Atlantic coast
events.summary: Sailing for England, John Cabot reached the coast of present-day Atlantic Canada in 1497, beginning the sustained European interest in the region's fisheries and lands that would follow.

figures.name: The Haudenosaunee (Iroquois Confederacy)
figures.blurb: A long-standing alliance of nations of the eastern woodlands, governed by the Great Law of Peace, whose settled agriculture, longhouse villages, and council-based governance shaped the politics of the northeast for centuries before and after contact.

pullquote.text: Indigenous peoples had lived across the land for thousands of years before any European set foot on it — and their nations endure.

eras.title: New France and the fur trade
eras.standfirst: French explorers and traders built a colony along the St. Lawrence anchored in the fur trade and in alliances with Indigenous nations, founding Quebec in 1608 and a society that endures in French Canada today.
body: French interest in the region began with Jacques Cartier, who explored the Gulf of St. Lawrence and sailed up the river in the 1530s, claiming the land for France. Lasting settlement came when Samuel de Champlain — later called the Father of New France — founded Quebec in 1608 as a fortified trading post at a strategic narrowing of the St. Lawrence. The colony's economy rested overwhelmingly on the fur trade, and its survival depended on alliances with Indigenous nations, especially the Huron-Wendat and Algonquian peoples, who supplied furs and knowledge in exchange for European goods.

New France grew slowly. French settlement clustered along the St. Lawrence, while traders, missionaries, and voyageurs pushed deep into the interior, building a network of posts that eventually reached the Great Lakes, the Mississippi, and the prairies. Catholic missionaries, especially the Jesuits, sought to convert Indigenous peoples and left detailed records of the encounter. The colony's reliance on Indigenous alliances also drew it into long conflict with the Haudenosaunee, who were tied to Dutch and later English trade.

By the eighteenth century New France was a small but deeply rooted society, with a distinctive French Catholic culture, seigneurial landholding, and a population of habitants farming the river lots of the St. Lawrence valley. But it was overshadowed by the far more populous British colonies to the south, and France and Britain's imperial rivalry would ultimately decide its fate. The Seven Years' War (1756–1763) brought that contest to a head, and New France fell to British arms.

events.title: Jacques Cartier explores the St. Lawrence
events.summary: Jacques Cartier explored the Gulf of St. Lawrence and claimed the region for France, opening the way for later French interest in the fur trade and settlement.

events.title: Champlain founds Quebec
events.summary: Samuel de Champlain established Quebec as a fortified trading post on the St. Lawrence, founding the colony of New France and anchoring the French fur trade in alliance with Indigenous nations.

events.title: Founding of Montreal
events.summary: French missionaries and settlers founded Ville-Marie, the future Montreal, as a mission and trading settlement that became a hub of the fur trade and a gateway to the interior.

events.title: Battle of the Plains of Abraham
events.summary: British forces defeated the French outside Quebec City in the decisive Battle of the Plains of Abraham, the turning point of the Seven Years' War in North America that led to the fall of New France.

figures.name: Samuel de Champlain
figures.role: Explorer and founder of New France
figures.blurb: French navigator and cartographer who founded Quebec in 1608 and built the alliances and trade that sustained the early colony, earning the title Father of New France.

pullquote.text: New France was a society built on the fur trade and on alliances with Indigenous nations along the great highway of the St. Lawrence.

eras.title: British conquest and colonial British North America
eras.standfirst: Britain's conquest of New France brought a French Catholic population under British rule, an influx of Loyalists after the American Revolution, rebellion and reform, and the gradual move toward responsible self-government.
body: The Treaty of Paris of 1763 ended the Seven Years' War and transferred New France to Britain, placing a large French-speaking, Roman Catholic population under a Protestant imperial power. Rather than assimilate them, Britain sought their loyalty: the Quebec Act of 1774 restored French civil law, guaranteed religious freedom for Catholics, and extended Quebec's boundaries — measures that helped keep the colony loyal but angered the Thirteen Colonies to the south on the eve of the American Revolution.

The American Revolution reshaped British North America. Tens of thousands of Loyalists, including Black Loyalists and Haudenosaunee allies, fled the new United States for the remaining British colonies, swelling the English-speaking population and prompting the division of Quebec into Upper Canada (largely English) and Lower Canada (largely French) in 1791. Settlement expanded, but so did Indigenous dispossession, as treaties and land cessions opened territory to newcomers.

Discontent with oligarchic colonial rule erupted in the Rebellions of 1837–38 in both Canadas. Britain responded by sending Lord Durham, whose 1839 report recommended uniting the Canadas and granting responsible government — rule by ministers accountable to an elected assembly. Responsible government was achieved in the late 1840s, and over the following decades the colonies developed the political maturity, and the practical need, to consider a federal union of British North America.

events.title: Treaty of Paris cedes New France to Britain
events.summary: The Treaty of Paris ended the Seven Years' War, with France ceding its North American mainland territories to Britain and bringing the French Catholic population of Canada under British rule.

events.title: Quebec Act
events.summary: The Quebec Act restored French civil law, guaranteed freedom of Catholic worship, and enlarged Quebec's boundaries, securing the loyalty of French Canadians while inflaming the Thirteen Colonies.

events.title: Constitutional Act divides the Canadas
events.summary: The Constitutional Act split Quebec into Upper Canada and Lower Canada, creating separate English- and French-majority colonies each with an elected assembly.

events.title: Rebellions in Upper and Lower Canada
events.summary: Armed uprisings against oligarchic colonial rule in both Canadas were suppressed but led to Lord Durham's report and, ultimately, to responsible government.

figures.name: Lord Durham (John George Lambton)
figures.blurb: British politician whose 1839 Report on the Affairs of British North America recommended uniting the Canadas and granting responsible government, shaping the path toward self-rule.

pullquote.text: Britain chose to accommodate, rather than assimilate, the French Catholic majority — a decision whose legacy still defines Canada.

eras.title: Confederation and nation-building across the continent
eras.standfirst: The British North America Act created the Dominion of Canada in 1867, a federation that swiftly expanded to the Pacific — bound together by a transcontinental railway and built on the dispossession of Indigenous nations.
body: After a series of conferences at Charlottetown and Quebec, the British North America Act came into force on 1 July 1867, uniting the Province of Canada (divided into Ontario and Quebec), Nova Scotia, and New Brunswick into the Dominion of Canada — a self-governing federation under the British Crown. Sir John A. Macdonald became the first prime minister. The new dominion was deliberately federal, balancing a strong central government with provincial powers and the distinct interests of French and English Canada.

Expansion was rapid. Canada purchased the vast Hudson's Bay Company territories (Rupert's Land) in 1870, prompting the Red River Resistance led by Louis Riel and the creation of Manitoba. British Columbia joined in 1871 on the promise of a transcontinental railway; Prince Edward Island followed in 1873. The Canadian Pacific Railway, completed in 1885 when the last spike was driven at Craigellachie, physically bound the country together and fulfilled Macdonald's National Dream of a nation stretching from the Atlantic to the Pacific.

This nation-building came at a devastating cost to Indigenous peoples. As settlers and the railway pushed west, the Crown signed the Numbered Treaties (1871–1921) with Indigenous nations, confining them to reserves and opening their lands to settlement and farming. The collapse of the bison herds brought starvation to the plains. The Indian Act of 1876 placed Indigenous peoples under sweeping federal control, and the residential school system — designed to assimilate Indigenous children by force — was expanded across the country. Métis grievances erupted in the North-West Resistance of 1885, after which Riel was hanged, deepening French–English division.

events.title: Confederation — the Dominion of Canada
events.summary: The British North America Act united Ontario, Quebec, Nova Scotia, and New Brunswick into the self-governing Dominion of Canada, with Sir John A. Macdonald as first prime minister.

events.title: Red River Resistance and Manitoba
events.summary: Métis led by Louis Riel resisted the transfer of Rupert's Land to Canada, securing rights that shaped the creation of the province of Manitoba in 1870.

events.title: The Indian Act
events.summary: The Indian Act consolidated federal control over the lives, lands, and governance of First Nations peoples, becoming a central instrument of assimilation and dispossession that remains in force in amended form.

events.title: Canadian Pacific Railway completed
events.summary: The driving of the last spike at Craigellachie completed the transcontinental Canadian Pacific Railway, binding the new nation together and fulfilling a promise made to British Columbia at Confederation.

events.title: North-West Resistance and the execution of Riel
events.summary: Métis and First Nations grievances on the prairies erupted in the North-West Resistance; its defeat and the hanging of Louis Riel deepened the divide between French and English Canada.

figures.name: Sir John A. Macdonald
figures.blurb: Leading architect of Confederation and Canada's first prime minister, who championed the transcontinental railway and the National Policy — and whose government built the residential school system and policies of Indigenous assimilation.

figures.name: Louis Riel
figures.blurb: Founder of Manitoba and leader of Métis resistance at Red River and in the North-West; executed for treason in 1885, he is now widely honoured as a defender of Métis rights and a Father of Confederation for Manitoba.

pullquote.text: Three provinces, by this Act, shall form and be One Dominion under the Name of Canada.

eras.title: The World Wars and growing autonomy
eras.standfirst: Service and sacrifice in two world wars forged a stronger sense of nationhood and won Canada a distinct voice in the world — autonomy formally recognised by the Statute of Westminster in 1931.
body: Canada entered the First World War in 1914 automatically when Britain declared war, but it fought as a national force whose achievements built a new sense of nationhood. The capture of Vimy Ridge in April 1917 — the first time all four Canadian divisions fought together — became a defining national symbol. The war also exposed deep divisions: the Conscription Crisis of 1917, when compulsory military service was imposed, set English Canada against French Canada and reshaped national politics. More than 60,000 Canadians died in the war.

Canada's wartime contribution earned it a seat at the Paris Peace Conference and separate membership in the League of Nations, marking its emergence as an international actor in its own right. This new autonomy was formalised by the Statute of Westminster in 1931, which granted Canada and the other dominions full legislative independence from the British Parliament. One major exception remained: by Canada's own request, the British North America Acts stayed under British amendment, because the federal government and provinces could not agree on a domestic amending formula.

The Great Depression of the 1930s brought mass unemployment and hardship, hitting the prairie wheat economy especially hard amid drought and collapsing prices. Recovery came with the Second World War, which Canada entered in 1939 — this time by its own parliamentary decision, a mark of its new autonomy. Canada mobilised on a vast scale, fielding major land, sea, and air forces, contributing to the Allied victory at sites such as the Normandy beaches in 1944, and emerging from the war as a more industrialised, confident, and independent nation.

events.title: Canada in the First World War
events.summary: Canada fought in the First World War as part of the British Empire but as a distinct national force; the costly victory at Vimy Ridge in 1917 became a symbol of emerging nationhood.

events.title: Conscription Crisis
events.summary: The imposition of compulsory military service deeply divided English and French Canada, becoming one of the most divisive episodes in Canadian political history.

events.title: Statute of Westminster
events.summary: The Statute of Westminster granted Canada legislative independence from the British Parliament, a landmark in its evolution toward full sovereignty.

events.title: Canada in the Second World War
events.summary: Canada entered the Second World War by its own parliamentary decision and mobilised on a vast scale, contributing major forces to the Allied victory and emerging more industrialised and autonomous.

figures.name: Sir Robert Borden
figures.role: Prime Minister during the First World War
figures.blurb: Led Canada through the First World War, introduced conscription in 1917, and pressed for Canada's separate recognition at the peace table and in the League of Nations.

pullquote.text: Vimy Ridge was the first time all four Canadian divisions fought together — a moment many came to see as the birth of a nation.

eras.title: Modern Canada — multiculturalism, patriation, and reconciliation
eras.standfirst: Post-war Canada built a welfare state, embraced official bilingualism and multiculturalism, patriated its constitution in 1982, and began a long reckoning with the harms of colonialism and the residential school system.
body: The decades after 1945 transformed Canada. Mass immigration from across Europe and, increasingly, from Asia, the Caribbean, Africa, and elsewhere reshaped its cities and society. A new social safety net took shape, including universal public health insurance (medicare) in the 1960s. In Quebec, the Quiet Revolution of the 1960s rapidly modernised and secularised the province and fuelled a sovereignty movement that would twice put Quebec independence to a referendum — defeated in 1980 and again, narrowly, in 1995.

Canada redefined its identity in law. The Official Languages Act of 1969 made English and French equal federal languages, and in 1971 Canada became the first country in the world to adopt multiculturalism as an official policy. The constitutional journey reached its climax with patriation in 1982: the Constitution Act, 1982, brought the constitution fully under Canadian control, entrenched the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, recognised the rights of Aboriginal peoples, and set out a domestic amending formula. Quebec, however, did not endorse the new constitution, and later attempts to bring it in — the Meech Lake and Charlottetown accords — failed.

Modern Canada has also confronted the darkest legacies of its founding. The Indian residential school system, run for more than a century, forcibly removed Indigenous children from their families, banned their languages and cultures, and subjected many to abuse and death. The Truth and Reconciliation Commission, which reported in 2015, documented these harms, described the system as 'cultural genocide,' and issued 94 Calls to Action. Reconciliation — including treaty rights, land claims, language revival, and accounting for the children who never returned home — remains one of the defining challenges of contemporary Canadian life.

Today Canada is a stable federal democracy and constitutional monarchy, a member of the G7, and one of the world's most diverse societies. It continues to negotiate its place in the world and the unfinished work of building a fairer relationship with the First Nations, Inuit, and Métis peoples on whose lands the country was built.

events.title: A new national flag and a maturing identity
events.summary: Canada adopted the red-and-white maple leaf flag in 1965, a symbol of a distinct national identity as the post-war country built a welfare state and universal health care.

events.title: Official multiculturalism policy
events.summary: Canada became the first country to adopt multiculturalism as official government policy, within a bilingual framework, recognising the contributions of diverse cultural communities.

events.title: Patriation of the Constitution
events.summary: The Constitution Act, 1982, patriated Canada's constitution, entrenched the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, recognised Aboriginal rights, and ended the need for British legislation — completing Canada's path to full sovereignty.

events.title: Quebec sovereignty referendum
events.summary: Quebec voters narrowly rejected sovereignty in the 1995 referendum, the closest of two votes on independence, underlining the enduring tension over the place of Quebec within Canada.

events.title: Truth and Reconciliation Commission reports
events.summary: After years of survivor testimony, the Truth and Reconciliation Commission documented the harms of the residential school system, described it as cultural genocide, and issued 94 Calls to Action for reconciliation.

figures.name: Pierre Elliott Trudeau
figures.role: Prime Minister and architect of patriation
figures.blurb: Introduced official multiculturalism in 1971 and championed the 1982 patriation of the constitution and the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, shaping the legal foundations of modern Canada.

pullquote.text: For over a century, the central goals of Canada's Aboriginal policy were... to cause Aboriginal peoples to cease to exist as distinct legal, social, cultural, religious, and racial entities in Canada.
pullquote.attribution: Truth and Reconciliation Commission of Canada, 2015

figures.name: Sir John A. Macdonald
figures.blurb: Principal architect of Confederation and Canada's first prime minister, who built the transcontinental railway and National Policy while also creating the structures of Indigenous assimilation.

figures.name: Samuel de Champlain
figures.blurb: Founded Quebec in 1608 and built the alliances and trade that sustained New France, earning the title Father of New France.

figures.name: Louis Riel
figures.role: Métis leader and founder of Manitoba
figures.blurb: Led Métis resistance at Red River and in the North-West; executed in 1885 and now honoured as a champion of Métis and minority rights.
